docs.title: ETo
docs.text:
Python package for calculating reference evapotranspiration (FAO 56)

ETo estimates reference evapotranspiration using the UN-FAO 56 paper methodology. It requires a minimum of T_min and T_max for daily estimates or T_mean and RH_mean for hourly estimates, and uses all available meteorological parameters to improve accuracy.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• FAO 56 Penman-Monteith — the international standard for reference ET estimation
  • Hargreaves method — simplified daily ET when limited data is available
  • Automatic parameter estimation — missing met parameters are estimated from available data using FAO 56 fallback chains
  • Quality tracking — est_val array records which parameters were estimated and at what quality level
  • Minimal dependencies — only requires NumPy

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
import numpy as np
from eto import ETo

# Prepare daily meteorological data
data = {
    'T_min': np.array([10.0, 12.0, 8.0, 11.0, 9.0]),
    'T_max': np.array([25.0, 28.0, 22.0, 26.0, 23.0]),
    'RH_min': np.array([40.0, 45.0, 35.0, 42.0, 38.0]),
    'RH_max': np.array([85.0, 90.0, 80.0, 88.0, 82.0]),
}

dates = np.arange('2020-01-01', '2020-01-06', dtype='datetime64[D]')

et = ETo(data, freq='D', z_msl=500, lat=-43.6, dates=dates)

eto_fao = et.eto_fao()       # np.ndarray of daily ETo (mm)
eto_har = et.eto_hargreaves() # np.ndarray of daily Hargreaves ETo (mm)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Installation — install ETo
  • Quick Start — complete walkthrough of a typical workflow
  • User Guide — detailed usage for daily and hourly estimation
  • API Reference — full class and method reference

docs.title: From PyPI
docs.text: pip install eto

docs.title: From Conda
docs.text: conda install -c mullenkamp eto

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python >= 3.8
  • NumPy

docs.title: Development Install
docs.text:
Clone the repository and install with uv:

git clone https://github.com/mullenkamp/ETo.git
cd ETo
uv sync

Run tests:

uv run pytest

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide walks through a complete ETo calculation using the included example dataset.

docs.title: Load the example data
docs.text:
ETo ships with a zipped CSV of daily meteorological data. We load it using the standard library:

import zipfile
import csv
import io
import numpy as np
from eto import ETo, datasets

# Get the path to the example dataset
path = datasets.get_path('example_daily')

# Load the CSV without pandas
with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
    name = z.namelist()[0]
    with z.open(name) as f:
        reader = csv.reader(io.TextIOWrapper(f))
        header = next(reader)
        rows = list(reader)

# Parse into numpy arrays
dates = np.array([row[0] for row in rows], dtype='datetime64[D]')
data = {}
for i, col in enumerate(header):
    if col == 'date':
        continue
    data[col] = np.array([float(row[i]) for row in rows], dtype=np.float64)

The example data contains columns: R_s, T_max, T_min, e_a.

docs.title: Run parameter estimation and calculate ETo
docs.text:
et = ETo(data, freq='D', z_msl=500, lat=-43.6, lon=172, TZ_lon=173, dates=dates)

This estimates all missing meteorological parameters (pressure, radiation, wind speed, etc.) from the available data.

docs.title: Get results
docs.text:
# FAO 56 Penman-Monteith ETo
eto_fao = et.eto_fao()
print(f"Mean daily ETo: {np.nanmean(eto_fao):.2f} mm")
print(f"Total ETo: {np.nansum(eto_fao):.2f} mm")

# Hargreaves ETo (simplified method)
eto_har = et.eto_hargreaves()
print(f"Mean daily Hargreaves ETo: {np.nanmean(eto_har):.2f} mm")

Both methods return a np.ndarray with the same length as the input data.

docs.title: Inspect estimated parameters
docs.text:
The full set of estimated parameters is available as a dictionary of arrays:

# All estimated parameter names
print(list(et.ts_param.keys()))

# Check a specific parameter
print(et.ts_param['R_n'][:5])  # Net radiation (first 5 values)

docs.title: Check estimation quality
docs.text:
The est_val array tracks which parameters were estimated:

print(et.est_val[:5])

A value of 0 means all parameters were measured directly. Higher values indicate more estimation was needed. See the Usage guide for details on interpreting these values.

docs.title: Reference Evapotranspiration (ETo)
docs.text:
Evapotranspiration (ET) is the combined process of evaporation from surfaces and transpiration from plant tissues. Since direct measurement of actual ET is difficult, indirect estimates are made using meteorological data.

The international standard for estimating reference ET was developed by the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (UN-FAO). The method uses the Penman-Monteith equation and provides guidelines for estimating missing meteorological parameters. It can work with as little data as minimum and maximum temperature, up to the full set of meteorological parameters.

The term "reference ET" (ETo) defines a specific vegetated surface (a hypothetical grass reference crop) by which the ET estimation is standardised. Different crop coefficients can then be applied to convert ETo to other crop types.

docs.title: FAO 56 Penman-Monteith
docs.text:
The FAO 56 Penman-Monteith equation is implemented in eto_fao(). It supports both daily and hourly time steps.

Extensive documentation on the methods and concepts can be found in the UN-FAO 56 paper.

docs.title: Parameter estimation
docs.text:
When input data is incomplete, the package estimates missing parameters using the fallback chains defined in FAO 56. Parameters are estimated in a specific order, and each estimation step is tracked in the est_val quality array. If a parameter is provided in the input data, the corresponding estimation is skipped entirely.

The parameters are estimated in the following order:

docs.title: Atmospheric pressure (P)
docs.text:
If P is not provided, it is estimated from station elevation (z_msl):

  1. P = 101.3 × ((293 - 0.0065 × z_msl) / 293)^5.26 (FAO Eq 7)

docs.title: Mean temperature (T_mean)
docs.text:
If T_mean is not provided (daily mode only — hourly requires it as input):

  1. T_mean = (T_max + T_min) / 2 (FAO Eq 9)

docs.title: Actual vapour pressure (e_a)
docs.text:
Hourly mode:

If e_a is not provided, it is estimated from RH_mean (which is required for hourly):

  1. e_a = e°(T_mean) × RH_mean / 100

Daily mode:

The daily fallback chain tries each method in order, stopping at the first that fills all missing values:

  1. From dew point temperature T_dew (FAO Eq 14) — best quality
  2. From RH_min + RH_max (FAO Eq 17) — e_a = (e°(T_min) × RH_max + e°(T_max) × RH_min) / 200
  3. From RH_max only (FAO Eq 18) — e_a = e°(T_min) × RH_max / 100
  4. From RH_mean only (FAO Eq 19) — e_a = RH_mean / 100 × (e°(T_max) + e°(T_min)) / 2
  5. From T_min (FAO Eq 48) — e_a = e°(T_min) — last resort, assumes dew point ≈ T_min

docs.title: Incoming solar radiation (R_s)
docs.text:
Extraterrestrial radiation (R_a) is always computed from latitude and day of year (FAO Eq 21/28). Then, if R_s is not provided:

  1. From sunshine hours n_sun using the Angstrom formula (FAO Eq 35) — R_s = (a_s + b_s × n/N) × R_a
  2. From temperature range using the Hargreaves radiation formula (FAO Eq 50) — R_s = K_rs × (T_max - T_min)^0.5 × R_a

docs.title: Net radiation (R_n)
docs.text:
If R_n is not provided, it is computed from R_s:

  1. R_n = R_ns - R_nl, where R_ns = (1 - albedo) × R_s (FAO Eq 38) and R_nl is the net outgoing longwave radiation (FAO Eq 39 for daily, Eq 40 for hourly)

docs.title: Soil heat flux (G)
docs.text:
If G is not provided:

  • Daily: G = 0 (FAO Eq 42 — negligible for daily time steps)
  • Hourly daytime (R_n > 0): G = 0.1 × R_n (FAO Eq 45)
  • Hourly nighttime (R_n ≤ 0): G = 0.5 × R_n (FAO Eq 46)

docs.title: Wind speed (U_2)
docs.text:
Wind speed at 2 m height is needed for the Penman-Monteith equation. If U_z is provided, it is converted:

  1. U_2 = U_z × 4.87 / ln(67.8 × z_u - 5.42) (FAO Eq 47)

If U_z is not provided, a default of U_2 = 2 m/s is used (FAO recommendation for missing wind data).

docs.title: Hargreaves
docs.text:
The Hargreaves equation is a simplified method for estimating ETo using only temperature data. It is implemented in eto_hargreaves() and is only valid for daily time steps.

The History and Evaluation of Hargreaves Evapotranspiration Equation provides a detailed description and background.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Allen, R. G., Pereira, L. S., Raes, D., & Smith, M. (1998). Crop evapotranspiration — Guidelines for computing crop water requirements. FAO Irrigation and drainage paper 56. FAO, Rome, 300(9), D05109.
  2. Hargreaves, G. and Allen, R. (2003). History and Evaluation of Hargreaves Evapotranspiration Equation. Journal of Irrigation and Drainage Engineering. Vol. 129, Issue 1 (February 2003).

docs.title: Migration from v1.x to v2.0
docs.text: ETo v2.0 replaces the pandas dependency with pure NumPy. This is a breaking API change.

docs.title: Summary of changes
docs.text:
• Input: pd.DataFrame with DatetimeIndex → dict[str, np.ndarray] + dates or day_of_year/hour
  • Internal state: self.ts_param is now dict[str, np.ndarray] (was pd.DataFrame); self.est_val is now np.ndarray (was pd.Series)
  • Output: eto_fao() and eto_hargreaves() return np.ndarray (was pd.Series or pd.DataFrame)
  • Removed: interp/maxgap parameters, tsreg() method, copy method
  • Dependency: numpy only (no pandas)

docs.title: Constructing the ETo object
docs.text:
v1.x (pandas)v2.0 (numpy) 
import pandas as pd
from eto import ETo

df = pd.read_csv('met_data.csv', parse_dates=True, index_col='date')
et = ETo(df, freq='D', z_msl=500, lat=-43.6)

import numpy as np
from eto import ETo

# Load your data into a dict of arrays
data = {
    'T_min': np.array([...]),
    'T_max': np.array([...]),
    'R_s': np.array([...]),
}
dates = np.array([...], dtype='datetime64[D]')

et = ETo(data, freq='D', z_msl=500, lat=-43.6, dates=dates)

docs.title: Getting results
docs.text:
v1.x (pandas)v2.0 (numpy) 
eto_series = et.eto_fao()           # pd.Series
total = eto_series.sum()             # NaN-unaware
total = eto_series.dropna().sum()    # NaN-safe

eto_array = et.eto_fao()             # np.ndarray
total = np.nansum(eto_array)         # NaN-safe

docs.title: Accessing estimated parameters
docs.text:
v1.x (pandas)v2.0 (numpy) 
et.ts_param['R_n']          # pd.Series
et.ts_param.loc[0, 'R_n']   # scalar
et.est_val.iloc[0]           # scalar

et.ts_param['R_n']          # np.ndarray
et.ts_param['R_n'][0]       # scalar
et.est_val[0]                # scalar

docs.title: Interpolation (removed)
docs.text:
The interp and maxgap parameters have been removed from eto_fao() and eto_hargreaves(). If you need interpolation, apply it to the output array directly:

# v2.0: post-process with scipy or your own logic
from scipy.interpolate import interp1d

eto = et.eto_fao()
valid = ~np.isnan(eto)
if valid.any():
    f = interp1d(np.where(valid)[0], eto[valid], bounds_error=False, fill_value=np.nan)
    eto_filled = f(np.arange(len(eto)))

docs.title: tsreg (removed)
docs.text: The tsreg() static method for time series regularisation has been removed. Use pandas or scipy directly if you need resampling.

docs.title: Pattern reference
docs.text: v1.x pandas pattern v2.0 numpy equivalent pd.DataFrame({'T_min': [...], ...}, index=dates) {'T_min': np.array([...]), ...} + dates=dates et.eto_fao().sum() np.nansum(et.eto_fao()) et.est_val.iloc[0] et.est_val[0] result.dropna() result[~np.isnan(result)] isinstance(result, pd.Series) isinstance(result, np.ndarray) et.ts_param['R_n'].values et.ts_param['R_n'] et.eto_fao(interp='linear') Removed — interpolate output yourself et.tsreg(ts, freq) Removed — use pandas/scipy directly

docs.title: Input data format
docs.text: ETo accepts meteorological data as a dict[str, np.ndarray] where each key is a parameter name and each value is a 1-D array. All arrays must have the same length.

docs.title: Supported parameters
docs.text: Key Description Unit R_n Net radiation MJ/m² R_s Incoming shortwave radiation MJ/m² G Net soil heat flux MJ/m² T_min Minimum temperature °C T_max Maximum temperature °C T_mean Mean temperature °C T_dew Dew point temperature °C RH_min Minimum relative humidity % RH_max Maximum relative humidity % RH_mean Mean relative humidity % n_sun Sunshine hours per day hours U_z Wind speed at height z m/s P Atmospheric pressure kPa e_a Actual vapour pressure kPa

docs.title: Minimum requirements
docs.text:
• Daily: T_min and T_max
  • Hourly: T_mean and either RH_mean or e_a

All other parameters are estimated from the available data using FAO 56 fallback chains.

docs.title: Temporal arguments
docs.text: ETo needs to know the day of year (and hour, for hourly data) for solar radiation calculations. There are two ways to provide this:

docs.title: Option 1: Pass dates
docs.text:
Pass a np.ndarray of datetime64 values. Day of year and hour are derived automatically:

dates = np.arange('2020-01-01', '2020-04-01', dtype='datetime64[D]')
et = ETo(data, freq='D', z_msl=500, lat=-43.6, dates=dates)

docs.title: Option 2: Pass day_of_year and hour
docs.text:
Pass integer arrays directly:

day_of_year = np.arange(1, 92)  # Jan 1 to Mar 31
et = ETo(data, freq='D', z_msl=500, lat=-43.6, day_of_year=day_of_year)

For hourly data, hour (0-23) is also required:

et = ETo(data, freq='h', z_msl=500, lat=-43.6, lon=172, TZ_lon=173,
         day_of_year=day_of_year, hour=hour)

docs.title: Daily estimation
docs.text:
Daily estimation requires only T_min and T_max:

import numpy as np
from eto import ETo

data = {
    'T_min': np.array([10.0, 12.0, 8.0]),
    'T_max': np.array([25.0, 28.0, 22.0]),
}
dates = np.arange('2020-01-01', '2020-01-04', dtype='datetime64[D]')

et = ETo(data, freq='D', z_msl=500, lat=-43.6, dates=dates)
eto = et.eto_fao()  # np.ndarray

Providing more parameters (e.g. R_s, RH_min, RH_max, U_z) reduces the amount of estimation and improves accuracy.

docs.title: Hourly estimation
docs.text:
Hourly estimation requires T_mean and either RH_mean or e_a. The lon and TZ_lon parameters are also needed for solar time calculations:

data = {
    'T_mean': np.full(48, 20.0),
    'RH_mean': np.full(48, 60.0),
    'R_s': r_s_array,  # hourly shortwave radiation
}
dates = np.arange('2020-06-15', '2020-06-17', dtype='datetime64[h]')

et = ETo(data, freq='h', z_msl=500, lat=-43.6, lon=172, TZ_lon=173, dates=dates)
eto = et.eto_fao()

Note

Hargreaves should not be used at sub-daily frequencies. Calling eto_hargreaves() on hourly data will raise a ValueError.

docs.title: Soil heat flux (G)
docs.text:
• Daily: G is set to 0 (FAO Eq 42)
  • Hourly: G = 0.1 × R_n during daytime (R_n > 0) and G = 0.5 × R_n at night (R_n ≤ 0) per FAO Eq 45/46

You can override this by providing G in the input data.

docs.title: Constructor parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description Default data dict[str, np.ndarray] of met data required freq 'D' for daily, 'h' for hourly 'D' z_msl Elevation above sea level (m) None lat Latitude (decimal degrees) None lon Longitude (decimal degrees) None TZ_lon Time zone centre longitude (decimal degrees) None z_u Wind measurement height (m) 2 K_rs R_s coefficient (0.16 inland, 0.19 coastal) 0.16 a_s R_s Angstrom coefficient 0.25 b_s R_s Angstrom coefficient 0.50 alb Albedo (0.23 for reference crop) 0.23 dates np.ndarray of datetime64 None day_of_year np.ndarray of int (1-366) None hour np.ndarray of int (0-23) None

docs.title: Estimation quality tracking
docs.text:
The est_val attribute is a np.ndarray of integers tracking which parameters were estimated. Each digit position represents a parameter:

Position (from right) Parameter Per-level increment 1 (ones) U_z 1 2 (tens) G 10 3 (hundreds) R_n 100 4 (thousands) R_s 1000 5 (ten-thousands) e_a 10000 6 (hundred-thousands) T_mean 100000 7 (millions) P 1000000

A value of 0 means all parameters were measured. Higher values indicate more estimation. For example, 1142111 means P was estimated (1), T_mean was estimated (1), e_a required 4 levels of fallback (4), R_s required 2 levels (2), R_n was estimated (1), G was estimated (1), and U_z was estimated (1).

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Both eto_fao() and eto_hargreaves() return a np.ndarray of ETo values in mm, with the same length as the input data. Extreme values outside [min_ETo, max_ETo] are set to NaN:

eto = et.eto_fao(max_ETo=15, min_ETo=0)  # defaults

docs.title: Legacy v1.x API (Pandas)
docs.text:
Warning

This page documents the v1.x API which used pandas DataFrames. If you are using ETo v2.0+, see the Usage guide instead.

docs.title: Installation (v1.x)
docs.text:
pip install eto==1.1.2

The v1.x series requires pandas as its core dependency.

docs.title: Initialising
docs.text:
The package is used via the main ETo class. It can be initialised without any parameters:

from eto import ETo, datasets
import pandas as pd

et1 = ETo()

docs.title: Parameter estimation
docs.text:
Input data must be a pd.DataFrame with a DatetimeIndex. Column names correspond to meteorological parameters.

ex1_path = datasets.get_path('example_daily')
tsdata = pd.read_csv(
    ex1_path, parse_dates=True,
    infer_datetime_format=True,
    index_col='date',
    compression='zip'
)

Run parameter estimation:

z_msl = 500
lat = -43.6
lon = 172
TZ_lon = 173
freq = 'D'

et1.param_est(tsdata, freq, z_msl, lat, lon, TZ_lon)
et1.ts_param.head()  # pd.DataFrame of all estimated parameters

docs.title: Calculate ETo
docs.text:
# FAO 56 Penman-Monteith
eto1 = et1.eto_fao()       # pd.Series
eto1.head()

# Hargreaves
eto2 = et1.eto_hargreaves() # pd.Series

docs.title: Interpolation (v1.x only)
docs.text:
v1.x supported built-in interpolation of the output:

# Returns a DataFrame with original and interpolated columns
eto_filled = et1.eto_fao(interp='linear', maxgap=15)

This feature was removed in v2.0.

docs.title: Time series regularisation (v1.x only)
docs.text:
The tsreg static method could resample and interpolate time series:

ts_regular = ETo.tsreg(ts, freq='D', interp='linear', maxgap=15)

This feature was removed in v2.0.

docs.title: Hourly estimation (v1.x)
docs.text:
For hourly data, the DataFrame needed an hourly DatetimeIndex:

# Resample daily to hourly using tsreg
tsdata_hourly = et1.tsreg(
    et1.ts_param[['R_s', 'T_mean', 'e_a']],
    'h', 'time'
)

et_hourly = ETo(tsdata_hourly, 'h', z_msl=500, lat=-43.6, lon=172, TZ_lon=173)
eto_hourly = et_hourly.eto_fao()

docs.title: Input parameters
docs.text:
The v1.x param_est function accepted a pd.DataFrame with these column names:

Column Description R_n Net radiation (MJ/m²) R_s Incoming shortwave radiation (MJ/m²) G Net soil heat flux (MJ/m²) T_min Minimum temperature (°C) T_max Maximum temperature (°C) T_mean Mean temperature (°C) T_dew Dew point temperature (°C) RH_min Minimum relative humidity (%) RH_max Maximum relative humidity (%) RH_mean Mean relative humidity (%) n_sun Sunshine hours per day U_z Wind speed at height z (m/s) P Atmospheric pressure (kPa) e_a Actual vapour pressure (kPa)

docs.title: Migrating to v2.0
docs.text: See the Migration guide for a detailed comparison and code examples.

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text:
All public classes and methods are available from the top-level eto package.

from eto import ETo

docs.title: ETo Class
docs.text: Name Description ETo Main class for parameter estimation and ETo calculation

docs.title: Methods
docs.text: Method Description param_est Estimate missing meteorological parameters eto_fao FAO 56 Penman-Monteith reference ET eto_hargreaves Hargreaves reference ET (daily only)

docs.title: eto.core.ETo
docs.text:
Bases: object

Class to handle the parameter estimation of metereological values and the calcuation of reference ET and similar ET methods.

This class can be either initiated with empty parameters or will initialise to the param_est function.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default data dict of str to np.ndarray, or None

Input meteorological data. Keys are parameter names (R_n, R_s, G, T_min, T_max, T_mean, T_dew, RH_min, RH_max, RH_mean, n_sun, U_z, P, e_a). All arrays must have the same length.

None freq str

Time frequency: 'D' for daily, 'H' or 'h' for hourly.

'D' day_of_year np.ndarray of int, or None

Day of year (1-366). Required if dates is not provided.

None hour np.ndarray of int, or None

Hour of day (0-23). Required for hourly frequency if dates is not provided.

None dates np.ndarray of datetime64, or None

Datetime array. Used to derive day_of_year and hour if they are not provided.

None

docs.title: eto.param_est.param_est(self, data, freq='D', z_msl=None, lat=None, lon=None, TZ_lon=None, z_u=2, K_rs=0.16, a_s=0.25, b_s=0.5, alb=0.23, day_of_year=None, hour=None)
docs.text:
Function to estimate the parameters necessary to calculate reference ET (ETo) from the FAO 56 paper <http://www.fao.org/docrep/X0490E/X0490E00.htm>_ using a minimum of T_min and T_max for daily estimates and T_mean and RH_mean for hourly, but optionally utilising the maximum number of available met parameters.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default data dict of str to np.ndarray

Input meteorological data.

required freq str

Time frequency string ('D' for daily, 'H'/'h' for hourly).

'D' z_msl float, int, or None

Elevation above mean sea level (m).

None lat float, int, or None

Latitude (decimal degrees).

None lon float, int, or None

Longitude (decimal degrees).

None TZ_lon float, int, or None

Longitude of the center of the time zone (decimal degrees).

None z_u float or int

Height of wind speed measurement (m). Default is 2 m.

2 K_rs float

Rs calc coefficient (0.16 inland, 0.19 coastal).

0.16 a_s float

Rs calc coefficient.

0.25 b_s float

Rs calc coefficient.

0.5 alb float

Albedo. Should be 0.23 for the reference crop.

0.23 day_of_year np.ndarray of int

Day of year (1-366).

None hour np.ndarray of int or None

Hour of day (0-23). Required for hourly frequency.

None

Returns:

Type Description None (populates self.ts_param and self.est_val)

docs.title: eto.methods.ETo.eto_fao(self, max_ETo=15, min_ETo=0)
docs.text:
Function to estimate reference ET (ETo) from the FAO 56 paper <http://www.fao.org/docrep/X0490E/X0490E00.htm> [1] using a minimum of T_min and T_max for daily estimates and T_mean and RH_mean for hourly, but optionally utilising the maximum number of available met parameters.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default max_ETo float or int

The max realistic value of ETo (mm).

15 min_ETo float or int

The min realistic value of ETo (mm).

0

Returns:

Type Description ndarray

Estimated ETo in mm.

References

.. [1] Allen, R. G., Pereira, L. S., Raes, D., & Smith, M. (1998). Crop evapotranspiration-Guidelines for computing crop water requirements-FAO Irrigation and drainage paper 56. FAO, Rome, 300(9), D05109.

docs.title: eto.methods.hargreaves.hargreaves(self, max_ETo=15, min_ETo=0)
docs.text:
Function to estimate Hargreaves ETo using a minimum of T_min and T_max, but optionally utilising the maximum number of available met parameters.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default max_ETo float or int

The max realistic value of ETo (mm).

15 min_ETo float or int

The min realistic value of ETo (mm).

0

Returns:

Type Description ndarray

Estimated ETo in mm.
